name: Samudra Manthan Pot
description: The Samudra Manthan Pot is a sacred vessel that emerged during the churning of the cosmic ocean (Samudra Manthan) in Hindu mythology. This churning was a joint effort between the Devas (gods) and Asuras (demons), who sought to obtain Amrita, the nectar of immortality. The pot contained the Amrita, making it an object of immense value and power. The pot itself is often depicted as a beautifully crafted golden or jewel-encrusted vessel. Its significance lies not only in its contents but also in the symbolism of the churning process, representing the struggle between good and evil and the attainment of enlightenment through perseverance. The acquisition of the Amrita from the pot led to a fierce battle between the Devas and Asuras, highlighting the eternal conflict between righteousness and wickedness. Possession of the Amrita Pot meant control over immortality, a power coveted by both sides. The pot, therefore, embodies the ultimate prize in the cosmic struggle, symbolizing the triumph of good over evil and the attainment of divine grace. The pot's emergence also signifies the creation of various other divine entities and objects, further cementing its importance in Hindu cosmology. The narrative surrounding the pot is central to understanding the concepts of dharma, karma, and the cyclical nature of creation and destruction.
subtitle: Vessel of Immortality and Cosmic Power
origin: Emerged during the churning of the cosmic ocean (Samudra Manthan)
currentLocation: Mythological realm; believed to be protected by divine forces, its physical manifestation is unattainable by mortals.

keyMyths.title: The Churning of the Ocean
keyMyths.description: The Samudra Manthan involved the Devas and Asuras churning the ocean to obtain Amrita. The pot containing the nectar emerged as one of the many treasures.

keyMyths.title: The Battle for Amrita
keyMyths.description: After the Amrita emerged, a fierce battle ensued between the Devas and Asuras for its possession. Vishnu, in the form of Mohini, tricked the Asuras to ensure the Devas consumed the nectar.

sources.author: Veda Vyasa
sources.description: Epic narrative containing the story of the Samudra Manthan.
